item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the cat, but I do feel that Addendum 2561-01 is a bit bigger than it needs to be. Just feels like unnecessary info is included in what is basically a recovery document. Think this could be tightened up a bit, but upvoted nonetheless.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Just to reiterate because this is a topic near and dear to this artist's heart:
If the modification involves the removal of a watermark, it is actually worse than if you only violated copyright by using or modifying the image without permission.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: It does not count. I went ahead and removed it as the timer had already ran down. PM incoming.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: If the modification in any way, shape, or form consists of removing a watermark, no.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Does it count if it was modified? I can remove the image if not

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: That image is not compliant with the site license. It has a copyright watermark on it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Image source: http://orig11.deviantart.net/688a/f/2017/078/1/0/profile_picture_by_shoutmilo-db2t0vb.jpg
Photoshopped from this image: http://i.dailymail.co.uk/i/pix/2017/02/09/03/3CFC18A700000578-0-image-a-15_1486611451081.jpg

http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-2077089/scp-announcements-26:theres-a-million-things-i-havent-done#post-2772884 Edited on announcement post

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed! Added exact times of silence, tweaked the interview, and added a nomenclature. I'll look for more sentences that I can make more clinical-sounding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's weird and strange - and that's a good thing. It caught my interest and made me read through and through. There's a few nitpicks I have with the clincal tone; such as making a reference to a species (domestic cat) but not giving us binomial nomenclature (felis catus). Otherwise, the containment and descriptions are enjoyable.

However, the interview… At the risk of giving conflicting advice I now think you've gone too far in the other direction. Now it seems the researcher is coddling the SCP, but that's my subjective opinion. Here's the line in particular I take issue with:

"You don't have to escape. You're safe here in containment. We just want to understand you. If you cooperate, we can better understand each other. That's what you want, right? To understand humans?"

My emphasis. I don't know it just….it just doesn't strike me as something a Foundation Dr. would say to a non-humanoid SCP, especially one so recently contained. I think you can strike a middle ground in tone with that line ("I'm sorry but we can't let that happen" or "You're not imprisoned, we're only trying to study you.") Remember, Foundation researchers and doctors have lied before/told half-truths to the SCP about the nature of their containment.

I have a few other nitpicks; having "repeating the initial phrase" in parenthesis is, to me, not needed. We can read, of course, that the SCP is doing precisely that. Also, as someone mentioned, if you want to give an interview transcript a feeling of time, in the (silence) portions, you should indicate a time. I.e: [SCP-XXXX is silent for 45 seconds].

All-in-all it held my interest and curiousity to the end. +1 from me. Nothing but nitpicks really stop me from enjoying the article, so well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cut the "without permission" sentence entirely, and put the frequency instead of expunging it. Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cut the "without permission" sentence entirely, and put the frequency instead of expunging it. Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this, and adding that it should be listed in the containment procedures with the radio itself. You can also take out the "never change it without permission" part: Anything in the containment procedures is automatically something you never change without a very good reason. It makes sense to include a side note like that if it's something that could require occasional deviation and/or is really, really important not to deviate from1, but in this case it wouldn't be world-ending and there's no real reason for it to ever be changed.

There's still some rough edges here and there you can sand out as you get more feedback, but overall much improved from the previous posting. +1

Footnotes
1. ie. "Hey, this month's supply shipment is running late; can we substitute chicken for duck on SCP-XXXX's meal plan, or will that summon the dreadlords or something…? …Oh. Really?…Okay, no chicken."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting and makes me curious. But I would just like to note that in my opinion the frequency on which to listen to the cat for should be disclosed.

If the listening frequency is ever changed by accident, how is anyone able to find it back without instructions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback man! I just tweaked the doctor's tone a bit so they aren't as harsh. I get what you mean, at first I felt like something was off about the tone of the interview but I couldn't pinpoint what it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate to always harp on this particular weirdly-common Foundation interviewer tic, but the doctor has been talking to a cat with a tv for a head, one that they didn't even know they could communicate with in the first place, for a minute and a half before he starts getting mad at it. (I read the conversation out loud and timed how long it took for him to start getting agitated, when he says "Don't change the subject.") It's pretty clear that this entity isn't something that's capable of fireside chats and pointed discourse; if anything, the interviewing doc should be much more supportive and engaged in order to get more information from this bizarre combination of animal and electronics. Conversations with this thing aren't going to be easy, and frankly, the researchers should be happy to be able to have them at all, so I'm not sure why they're so angry about it.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: This is my first SCP article ever, and it's gone through about 60+ revisions since the original draft. Thanks to Spudkin, Shaggydredlocks, Rioghail, canaryfarmer and LordStonefish for giving me helpful critiques and leading the article in the right direction. If there are still errors in this draft, I can fix them here (or if there is a big error that I am missing, I can delete the page and go back to the Draft forums to rework it). Please let me know if you don't think its ready for the mainlist, and why. Thanks for reading : )
